facts.text: Snow White and the Seven Dwarfs (1937) was Walt Disney's first full-length animated feature and cost $1.5 million to produce — a staggering sum at the time.
Walt Disney originally envisioned Snow White as a silent film before deciding on a full musical production.
The Lion King (1994) was not considered a prestige project at Disney — many animators chose to work on Pocahontas instead.
The wildebeest stampede scene in The Lion King took roughly three years to animate using a combination of hand-drawn and CGI techniques.
The Little Mermaid (1989) is widely credited with sparking the Disney Renaissance era of animated films.
Jodi Benson recorded Ariel's voice for The Little Mermaid in a darkened studio so she could feel like she was underwater.
Aladdin's Genie was specifically written with Robin Williams in mind, and the studio created a special animation reel to convince him to take the role.
Robin Williams ad-libbed so much during Aladdin recording sessions that the studio reportedly had over 16 hours of material.
Beauty and the Beast (1991) was the first animated film ever nominated for the Academy Award for Best Picture.
The ballroom scene in Beauty and the Beast was one of the earliest uses of CGI backgrounds combined with hand-drawn characters in a Disney film.
Frozen (2013) became the highest-grossing animated film of all time at the point of its release, earning over $1.28 billion worldwide.
The song 'Let It Go' from Frozen was originally written as a villain's anthem before the filmmakers reimagined Elsa as a sympathetic character.
Idina Menzel auditioned for Tangled before being cast as Elsa in Frozen.
Frozen II (2019) surpassed its predecessor at the box office, grossing over $1.45 billion.
Moana (2016) featured music by Lin-Manuel Miranda, Opetaia Foa'i, and Mark Mancina, blending Polynesian and Broadway styles.
Auli'i Cravalho was just 14 years old when she was cast as the voice of Moana.
The ocean in Moana was treated as a character itself, requiring entirely new animation technology to give water a personality.
Toy Story (1995) was the first feature-length film entirely animated using CGI.
Early test footage of Toy Story featured a much meaner version of Woody, which nearly got the film canceled by Disney.
Tom Hanks recorded his lines for Woody in Toy Story by acting out scenes physically in the recording booth to convey emotion.
Toy Story 2 was originally planned as a direct-to-video sequel before Pixar decided the story was strong enough for theaters.
Toy Story 3 (2010) grossed over $1 billion worldwide and was nominated for Best Picture at the Oscars.
Toy Story 4 (2019) won the Academy Award for Best Animated Feature Film.
Finding Nemo (2003) became the best-selling DVD of all time in its first year of release, with over 40 million copies sold.
After Finding Nemo's release, clownfish sales reportedly increased by 40%, raising concerns about wild populations.
Finding Dory (2016) earned $1.029 billion, making it one of the highest-grossing animated sequels.
Up (2009) was the first animated film and first 3D film to open the Cannes Film Festival.
The opening montage of Up, depicting Carl and Ellie's life together, is frequently cited as one of the most emotional sequences in film history.
Inside Out (2015) took roughly five years to develop because director Pete Docter wanted the emotions and psychology to be as accurate as possible.
Pixar consulted with psychologists and neuroscientists to ensure the emotional concepts in Inside Out were scientifically grounded.
Inside Out 2 (2024) became the highest-grossing animated film of all time, surpassing Frozen II.
WALL-E (2008) has almost no dialogue for its first 40 minutes, relying on visual storytelling and sound design.
WALL-E's design was partly inspired by binoculars, and his name stands for Waste Allocation Load Lifter Earth-class.
Coco (2017) was the first film with a nine-figure budget to feature an almost entirely Latino cast.
Coco took six years to develop, during which Pixar's team made multiple research trips to Mexico to understand Día de los Muertos traditions.
The Incredibles (2004) was the first Pixar film to focus entirely on human characters rather than toys, bugs, fish, or monsters.
Brad Bird wrote and directed The Incredibles and also voiced the character Edna Mode.
Incredibles 2 (2018) earned over $1.24 billion, making it one of Pixar's highest-grossing films.
Ratatouille (2007) required Pixar to develop new hair simulation software, as Remy the rat had over 1.1 million individual hairs.
Monsters, Inc. (2001) pushed animation technology forward — Sulley's fur contained over 2.3 million individually animated hairs.
A Bug's Life (1998) and DreamWorks' Antz were released the same year, sparking one of Hollywood's most famous animation rivalries.
Cars (2006) was inspired by director John Lasseter's cross-country road trip with his family along Route 66.
Brave (2012) was Pixar's first film with a female protagonist, featuring Merida as a Scottish princess.
Soul (2020) was the first Pixar film to feature an African American lead character.
Turning Red (2022) was Pixar's first feature directed solely by a woman, Domee Shi.
Luca (2021) was inspired by director Enrico Casarosa's childhood summers on the Italian Riviera.
Shrek (2001) won the first-ever Academy Award for Best Animated Feature Film.
Mike Myers re-recorded all of Shrek's dialogue in a Scottish accent after initially performing it with a Canadian accent.
Shrek was originally developed by Steven Spielberg as a traditionally animated film before DreamWorks shifted it to CGI.
Shrek 2 (2004) was the third-highest-grossing film of 2004 and remains one of the highest-grossing animated films ever.
How to Train Your Dragon (2010) was based on Cressida Cowell's children's book series of the same name.
The bond between Hiccup and Toothless in How to Train Your Dragon was animated by studying how people interact with cats.
Kung Fu Panda (2008) was so well-received in China that it sparked a national debate about why China hadn't produced a similar film.
Jack Black performed many of Po's martial arts movements himself in a motion-capture studio for Kung Fu Panda.
Madagascar (2005) was DreamWorks' fifth animated feature and launched a franchise grossing over $2.2 billion.
The Penguins of Madagascar became so popular they got their own TV series and feature film.
DreamWorks Animation was founded by Steven Spielberg, Jeffrey Katzenberg, and David Geffen in 1994.
The Prince of Egypt (1998) was one of DreamWorks' earliest animated films and featured a score by Hans Zimmer.
Puss in Boots: The Last Wish (2022) was praised for its unique painterly animation style inspired by classic fairy tale illustrations.
Spirited Away (2001) won the Academy Award for Best Animated Feature, the only hand-drawn and non-English-language film to do so.
Spirited Away held the record as Japan's highest-grossing film for nearly 20 years until Demon Slayer: Mugen Train surpassed it in 2020.
Hayao Miyazaki founded Studio Ghibli with Isao Takahata in 1985, creating some of the most celebrated animated films in history.
My Neighbor Totoro (1988) was a box office disappointment initially but became a cultural icon and Studio Ghibli's mascot.
The character Totoro was inspired by a mix of owls, cats, and tanuki from Japanese folklore.
Princess Mononoke (1997) was the highest-grossing film in Japan until Titanic surpassed it the same year.
Howl's Moving Castle (2004) was based on the novel by British author Diana Wynne Jones and was nominated for an Academy Award.
Kiki's Delivery Service (1989) was adapted from a novel by Eiko Kadono and became a beloved coming-of-age story worldwide.
Castle in the Sky (1986) was the first official Studio Ghibli film ever produced.
Ponyo (2008) featured entirely hand-drawn animation with over 170,000 individual frames — Miyazaki refused to use CGI.
The Wind Rises (2013) departed from Miyazaki's typical fantasy themes, telling a fictionalized biography of aircraft designer Jiro Horikoshi.
The Boy and the Heron (2023) won the Academy Award for Best Animated Feature and was Miyazaki's return from retirement.
Grave of the Fireflies (1988) is widely considered one of the most powerful anti-war films ever made, animated or otherwise.
Studio Ghibli's films have generated over $5 billion in worldwide revenue.
The Nightmare Before Christmas (1993) took over three years to film using stop-motion, with animators capturing 24 frames for each second of footage.
Jack Skellington required around 400 different heads to portray all his various expressions in The Nightmare Before Christmas.
Tim Burton conceived The Nightmare Before Christmas while working as an animator at Disney in the early 1980s.
Wallace & Gromit creator Nick Park has won four Academy Awards, making him one of the most decorated animators in Oscar history.
The Wrong Trousers (1993), a Wallace & Gromit short, is often ranked among the greatest animated films ever made.
Coraline (2009) used over 150 handmade sets and required 18 months of stop-motion filming.
Coraline's character had over 200,000 possible facial expressions thanks to 3D-printed replacement faces.
Kubo and the Two Strings (2016) featured a 16-foot-tall skeleton puppet — the largest stop-motion puppet ever built.
Laika Studios, which made Coraline, ParaNorman, and Kubo, pioneered the use of 3D printing in stop-motion animation.
Chicken Run (2000) by Aardman Animations remains the highest-grossing stop-motion animated film of all time.
Fantastic Mr. Fox (2009) was directed by Wes Anderson using real fur puppets and an unusually low frame rate for a distinctive jittery style.
James and the Giant Peach (1996) combined live action with stop-motion animation sequences.
The Boxtrolls (2014) had a scene with over 55 puppets on screen simultaneously, a massive undertaking in stop-motion.
Isle of Dogs (2018) by Wes Anderson featured over 1,000 handcrafted puppets and used real cotton for clouds and water effects.
Dragon Ball, created by Akira Toriyama, has been one of the most influential anime franchises since its debut in 1986.
Dragon Ball Z popularized anime in the West during the 1990s and inspired an entire generation of animators and storytellers.
Naruto became one of the best-selling manga series of all time, with over 250 million copies sold worldwide.
The 'Naruto run' became a viral internet meme and was famously referenced during the 2019 Storm Area 51 event.
Attack on Titan's final season shattered streaming records on platforms like Crunchyroll and Funimation.
Attack on Titan was inspired by creator Hajime Isayama's experience of feeling trapped in his rural hometown.
Demon Slayer: Mugen Train (2020) became the highest-grossing film in Japanese box office history, surpassing Spirited Away.
Demon Slayer's animation by studio Ufotable blends traditional 2D animation with CGI to create its distinctive visual style.
One Piece has been serialized in Weekly Shōnen Jump since 1997 and holds the Guinness World Record for most copies published for a single comic series.
One Piece creator Eiichiro Oda originally planned the series to run for five years — it has been running for over 25.
Akira (1988) cost over $11 million to produce, making it the most expensive anime film at the time, and it revolutionized Western perceptions of anime.
Ghost in the Shell (1995) heavily influenced The Matrix, with the Wachowskis showing the film to producers to explain their vision.
Neon Genesis Evangelion (1995) deconstructed the mecha genre and became one of the most analyzed anime series of all time.
Your Name (2016) by Makoto Shinkai became the highest-grossing anime film worldwide at the time, earning over $380 million.
Studio Ghibli's films became available on Netflix in most countries in 2020, introducing Miyazaki's work to a massive new audience.
Sailor Moon popularized the magical girl genre worldwide and became a cultural phenomenon in the 1990s.
Cowboy Bebop is often cited as a gateway anime for Western audiences and is credited with expanding anime's global reach.
Fullmetal Alchemist: Brotherhood is consistently ranked as one of the highest-rated anime series on MyAnimeList.
Death Note (2006) demonstrated that anime could succeed as a psychological thriller without action-heavy sequences.
My Hero Academia drew inspiration from both traditional shōnen anime and Western superhero comics like those from Marvel and DC.
Cel animation involves drawing each frame on a transparent celluloid sheet, which is then photographed over a painted background.
Before CGI, Disney animators used a multiplane camera to create depth by layering multiple painted glass panels at varying distances.
The multiplane camera was first used in the 1937 short The Old Mill and later became essential for Snow White's production.
Rotoscoping, invented by Max Fleischer in 1915, involves tracing over live-action footage frame by frame to create realistic animation.
Early Disney films like Snow White used rotoscoping extensively for human characters while animals were animated freehand.
The xerography process, introduced in 101 Dalmatians (1961), allowed animators to skip the inking step by photocopying pencil drawings directly onto cels.
Pixar's RenderMan software has been used for visual effects in nearly every Oscar-winning VFX film since the mid-1990s.
The CGI revolution in animation began with Pixar's short film Luxo Jr. (1986), which was the first CGI film nominated for an Academy Award.
Each frame of a modern Pixar film can take anywhere from 6 to 90 hours to render, depending on complexity.
Toy Story required approximately 800,000 machine hours of rendering, while later Pixar films required hundreds of millions.
Motion capture technology allows animators to record real human movements and apply them to animated characters.
The Polar Express (2004) was one of the first animated films to use extensive performance capture technology.
Spider-Man: Into the Spider-Verse (2018) pioneered a groundbreaking animation style that combined CGI with comic book techniques like halftone dots and variable frame rates.
Into the Spider-Verse animated different characters at different frame rates — Miles at 12 fps and Peter at 24 fps — to show Miles's inexperience.
Spider-Man: Across the Spider-Verse (2023) used over 240 unique art styles to represent different dimensions.
Ray tracing technology has enabled animated films to achieve photorealistic lighting by simulating how light interacts with objects.
Deep learning and AI tools are increasingly being used in animation for tasks like in-betweening and background generation.
Puss in Boots: The Last Wish pioneered a style mixing CGI with hand-painted textures, using as few as 8 frames per second for stylistic effect.
Mel Blanc, known as the Man of a Thousand Voices, voiced Bugs Bunny, Daffy Duck, Porky Pig, and many other Looney Tunes characters.
Tom Hanks voiced Woody in all four Toy Story films spanning 24 years from 1995 to 2019.
Ellen DeGeneres's performance as Dory in Finding Nemo was so popular that a sequel centered on her character was produced 13 years later.
Eddie Murphy's improvisation as Donkey in Shrek made the character far more prominent than originally planned.
Cameron Diaz, Mike Myers, and Eddie Murphy all returned for multiple Shrek sequels, establishing one of animation's most iconic voice casts.
James Earl Jones voiced Mufasa in both the 1994 animated Lion King and the 2019 CGI remake.
Ming-Na Wen voiced Mulan in the 1998 film while Lea Salonga provided Mulan's singing voice — the same arrangement used for Jasmine in Aladdin.
Dwayne 'The Rock' Johnson voiced Maui in Moana and performed the song 'You're Welcome' himself.
Billy Crystal was originally offered the role of Buzz Lightyear in Toy Story but turned it down — he later called it the biggest mistake of his career.
Tim Allen voiced Buzz Lightyear in the original Toy Story films, while Chris Evans voiced the character in the spin-off Lightyear (2022).
Mark Hamill has had a prolific career as a voice actor, most famously voicing the Joker in Batman: The Animated Series.
Tara Strong has voiced hundreds of animated characters including Timmy Turner, Bubbles, Raven, and Twilight Sparkle.
Keith David voiced Goliath in Gargoyles and Dr. Facilier in The Princess and the Frog, bringing a rich baritone to animated villainy.
Phil Harris voiced both Baloo in The Jungle Book (1967) and Little John in Robin Hood (1973) for Disney.
Amy Poehler voiced Joy in Inside Out and its sequel, delivering the character's relentlessly upbeat energy.
The first Academy Award for Best Animated Feature was given in 2002, and Shrek won the inaugural award.
Spirited Away won Best Animated Feature at the 75th Academy Awards in 2003, the only non-English-language film to win the category.
Frozen won both Best Animated Feature and Best Original Song at the 2014 Academy Awards.
Pixar has won 11 Academy Awards for Best Animated Feature — more than any other studio.
Walt Disney holds the record for the most Academy Awards won by an individual, with 22 competitive Oscars.
The Lion King (2019 remake) grossed over $1.65 billion worldwide, making it the highest-grossing animated remake.
Frozen's worldwide gross of $1.28 billion made it the first animated film to cross the billion-dollar mark since Toy Story 3.
Incredibles 2 set a domestic opening weekend record for animated films when it debuted with $182.7 million in 2018.
Minions (2015) grossed over $1.16 billion worldwide, proving the appeal of Despicable Me's breakout characters.
The Despicable Me franchise has generated over $4.6 billion in worldwide box office revenue.
Zootopia (2016) earned $1.024 billion and won the Academy Award for Best Animated Feature.
The Super Mario Bros. Movie (2023) earned over $1.36 billion, becoming the second-highest-grossing animated film at the time.
The Simpsons debuted as shorts on The Tracey Ullman Show in 1987 before getting its own series in 1989.
The Simpsons holds the record for the longest-running American animated program and prime-time scripted series.
Homer Simpson's famous 'D'oh!' was added to the Oxford English Dictionary in 2001.
The Simpsons has accurately predicted numerous real-world events, from Disney acquiring 21st Century Fox to smartwatches.
Dan Castellaneta, who voices Homer Simpson, also voices numerous other characters on the show including Krusty the Clown and Grampa Simpson.
South Park's pilot episode was made using paper cutout stop-motion animation before the show shifted to digital methods.
Trey Parker and Matt Stone typically write, direct, and produce each South Park episode in just six days.
South Park debuted on Comedy Central in 1997 and became one of the most culturally influential animated series.
Family Guy was canceled twice by Fox before being revived due to strong DVD sales and Adult Swim reruns.
Seth MacFarlane voices Peter, Stewie, Brian, and Quagmire on Family Guy, performing all four characters in the same recording sessions.
Bob's Burgers (2011) has been praised for its wholesome family dynamics, a contrast to more cynical animated sitcoms.
Bob's Burgers: The Movie was released in 2022 after pandemic-related delays and was both a critical and fan favorite.
Bluey became a global phenomenon, with its Australian creators winning an International Emmy Kids Award.
Bluey episodes are only about seven minutes long but have been acclaimed for their emotional depth and appeal to both children and adults.
SpongeBob SquarePants was created by marine science educator Stephen Hillenburg and premiered on Nickelodeon in 1999.
SpongeBob SquarePants has generated over $13 billion in merchandise revenue, making it one of the most profitable animated franchises.
The SpongeBob SquarePants Movie (2004) earned $140 million worldwide and expanded the show's cultural footprint.
Avatar: The Last Airbender is often considered one of the greatest animated series of all time despite being marketed as a children's show.
Futurama was created by The Simpsons creator Matt Groening and has been revived multiple times due to its devoted fanbase.
Rick and Morty's pilot was based on a crude parody of Back to the Future that co-creator Justin Roiland made for a film festival.
Archer blends spy-genre parody with sophisticated humor and has won three Primetime Emmy nominations.
King of the Hill ran for 13 seasons and was co-created by Mike Judge, who also created Beavis and Butt-Head.
Adventure Time (2010–2018) was credited with ushering in a new golden age of animated television for kids and adults alike.
Steven Universe was the first Cartoon Network series created solely by a woman, Rebecca Sugar.
Gravity Falls ran for only two seasons but developed a massive cult following for its intricate lore and mystery-driven storytelling.
Batman: The Animated Series (1992) redefined superhero animation with its noir-influenced art style and mature storytelling.
Harley Quinn was originally created for Batman: The Animated Series and became so popular she was incorporated into DC Comics canon.
Scooby-Doo has been continuously produced in various forms since 1969, making it one of the longest-running animated franchises.
The Flintstones (1960) was the first animated program to air during prime time on American television.
Tom and Jerry shorts have won seven Academy Awards for Best Animated Short Film.
Looney Tunes and Merrie Melodies shorts were originally created by Warner Bros. to promote their music catalog.
Walt Disney was born on December 5, 1901, in Chicago, Illinois.
Walt Disney opened Disneyland in Anaheim, California, on July 17, 1955, calling it 'the happiest place on Earth.'
Walt Disney's first successful cartoon character was Oswald the Lucky Rabbit, but he lost the rights to Universal Studios.
Mickey Mouse first appeared in the short Steamboat Willie on November 18, 1928, one of the first cartoons with synchronized sound.
Walt Disney won an honorary Academy Award for Snow White in 1939, receiving one full-sized Oscar and seven miniature ones.
Fantasia (1940) was a commercial failure on its initial release but is now considered one of the greatest animated films ever made.
Disney's Bambi (1942) faced delays partly because animators spent months studying real deer to perfect their movements.
Sleeping Beauty (1959) was the last Disney film to use hand-inked cels before transitioning to the xerography process.
The Disney Vault was a marketing strategy where classic films were released on home video for limited periods before being locked away.
Cinderella (1950) saved the Walt Disney studio from near-bankruptcy after a string of underperforming films in the 1940s.
Peter Pan (1953) took 14 years to develop, as Walt Disney first acquired the rights in 1939.
Lady and the Tramp (1955) was the first Disney animated feature filmed in CinemaScope widescreen format.
One Hundred and One Dalmatians (1961) used the new xerography process, which gave the animation a sketchier, more modern look.
The Jungle Book (1967) was the last animated film personally supervised by Walt Disney before his death in December 1966.
The Rescuers (1977) outperformed Star Wars in several international markets during its theatrical run.
The Great Mouse Detective (1986) was the first Disney animated feature to use CGI, for the clock tower gears in the climax.
Who Framed Roger Rabbit (1988) seamlessly blended live action with animation and won three Academy Awards.
Aladdin (1992) was the first animated film to gross over $200 million at the domestic box office.
Pocahontas (1995) featured the Academy Award-winning song 'Colors of the Wind' by Alan Menken and Stephen Schwartz.
The Hunchback of Notre Dame (1996) tackled unusually dark themes for a Disney film, including religious fanaticism and genocide.
Hercules (1997) featured gospel-inspired musical numbers by Alan Menken, a departure from Disney's typical Broadway style.
Mulan (1998) was partly produced at Disney's animation studio in Florida and featured extensive cultural consultation.
Tarzan (1999) featured Phil Collins performing the entire soundtrack, which won the Academy Award for Best Original Song.
Lilo & Stitch (2002) was primarily produced at Disney's Florida studio and featured watercolor backgrounds, a rarity for Disney at the time.
The Emperor's New Groove (2000) was completely reworked from a serious epic called Kingdom of the Sun into a buddy comedy.
Tangled (2010) cost approximately $260 million to produce, making it one of the most expensive animated films ever.
Wreck-It Ralph (2012) featured cameos from over 180 real video game characters.
Big Hero 6 (2014) was Disney's first animated film based on a Marvel Comics property.
Encanto (2021) featured the hit song 'We Don't Talk About Bruno,' which reached No. 1 on the Billboard Hot 100.
Wish (2023) was created to celebrate Disney Animation's 100th anniversary.
Snow White and the Seven Dwarfs premiered on December 21, 1937, at the Carthay Circle Theatre in Los Angeles.
The Little Mermaid was released on November 17, 1989, marking the start of the Disney Renaissance.
Beauty and the Beast was released in the United States on November 22, 1991.
Aladdin was released on November 25, 1992.
The Lion King premiered on June 15, 1994, and went on to earn over $968 million worldwide.
Toy Story was released on November 22, 1995, changing animation history forever.
Spirited Away was released in Japan on July 20, 2001.
Finding Nemo was released on May 30, 2003, and became the year's second-highest-grossing film.
Frozen was released on November 27, 2013, and became a global cultural phenomenon.
Inside Out was released on June 19, 2015.
Coco was released in the United States on November 22, 2017.
Spider-Man: Into the Spider-Verse was released on December 14, 2018, and won the Academy Award for Best Animated Feature.
Anime as a distinct art form emerged in Japan in the early 20th century, with the earliest known Japanese animation dating to 1917.
Osamu Tezuka, creator of Astro Boy, is often called the 'God of Manga' and his work laid the foundation for modern anime.
Astro Boy (1963) was one of the first anime television series and helped establish the conventions of the medium.
Studio Trigger, founded by former Gainax members, created Kill la Kill and Promare, known for their over-the-top action and visual flair.
Weathering with You (2019) by Makoto Shinkai grossed over $193 million, cementing him as one of anime's top directors.
Jujutsu Kaisen 0: The Movie (2021) grossed over $196 million worldwide, demonstrating anime's growing global box office power.
The anime industry was valued at over $25 billion in 2023, with streaming platforms driving much of the growth.
Crunchyroll, the largest anime streaming platform, had over 13 million paid subscribers by 2024.
Pokémon the Movie: Mewtwo Strikes Back (1998) launched the Pokémon film franchise and earned $172 million worldwide.
The first feature-length animated film ever made was El Apóstol (1917), an Argentine political satire, but no prints of it survive today.
Gertie the Dinosaur (1914), created by Winsor McCay, is considered one of the earliest examples of character animation.
Felix the Cat, created in 1919, was the first animated character to achieve a level of popularity sufficient for merchandise.
Betty Boop was one of the most popular animated characters of the 1930s and was modeled partly on singer Helen Kane.
Fleischer Studios, creators of Betty Boop and Popeye, were Walt Disney's primary competitors in the 1930s.
The rotoscoping technique was used extensively in Ralph Bakshi's adult-oriented animated films like Fritz the Cat (1972) and Heavy Traffic (1973).
Fritz the Cat (1972) was the first animated film to receive an X rating from the MPAA.
Yellow Submarine (1968) featured the Beatles and became a landmark in psychedelic animation art.
Watership Down (1978) shocked many parents with its graphic depiction of violence in what appeared to be a children's film about rabbits.
The Secret of NIMH (1982) was Don Bluth's directorial debut after he left Disney, and it became a cult classic.
Don Bluth directed several beloved 1980s animated films including An American Tail (1986) and The Land Before Time (1988).
The Land Before Time spawned 13 direct-to-video sequels, making it one of the most extended animated franchises.
An American Tail: Fievel Goes West (1991) was produced by Steven Spielberg's Amblin Entertainment.
Anastasia (1997) was Fox Animation Studios' most successful film and competed directly with Disney releases.
The Iron Giant (1999) was a box office bomb but became a beloved classic after its home video release.
Brad Bird directed The Iron Giant before going on to direct The Incredibles and Ratatouille at Pixar.
Persepolis (2007) was an autobiographical animated film about growing up during the Iranian Revolution, nominated for an Academy Award.
Waltz with Bashir (2008) was an animated documentary about the 1982 Lebanon War and was nominated for an Academy Award for Best Foreign Language Film.
The Red Turtle (2016) was a co-production between Studio Ghibli and a European studio, told entirely without dialogue.
Wolfwalkers (2020) by Cartoon Saloon continued the Irish studio's tradition of stunningly hand-drawn animated films.
Cartoon Saloon's The Secret of Kells (2009), Song of the Sea (2014), and Wolfwalkers (2020) were all nominated for Best Animated Feature.
The Breadwinner (2017) by Cartoon Saloon told the story of a girl disguising herself as a boy in Taliban-controlled Afghanistan.
Flee (2021) was the first film ever nominated for Best Animated Feature, Best Documentary Feature, and Best International Feature Film simultaneously.
I Lost My Body (2019) was a French animated film nominated for Best Animated Feature, praised for its unique visual style and storytelling.
Klaus (2019) was Netflix's first original animated feature film and was nominated for an Academy Award.
The Mitchells vs. the Machines (2021) was produced by Phil Lord and Chris Miller and became one of Netflix's most-watched animated films.
Guillermo del Toro's Pinocchio (2022) was a stop-motion musical set during Mussolini's Italy and won the Academy Award for Best Animated Feature.
Robot Dreams (2023) was a Spanish animated film told entirely without dialogue that earned a Best Animated Feature nomination.
Alan Menken composed the music for The Little Mermaid, Beauty and the Beast, Aladdin, Pocahontas, and many other Disney films, winning eight Academy Awards.
The Sherman Brothers wrote songs for Mary Poppins, The Jungle Book, and The Aristocats, becoming Disney's most prolific songwriting duo.
Randy Newman has composed scores for every Toy Story film and wrote 'You've Got a Friend in Me,' which became the franchise's signature song.
Hans Zimmer composed the score for The Lion King, incorporating African choral music and winning an Academy Award.
Elton John co-wrote songs for The Lion King including 'Circle of Life' and 'Can You Feel the Love Tonight.'
Phil Collins won the Academy Award for Best Original Song for 'You'll Be in My Heart' from Tarzan.
Lin-Manuel Miranda wrote songs for both Moana and Encanto, earning Oscar nominations for both.
The songs from Encanto dominated music charts, with 'We Don't Talk About Bruno' becoming Disney's biggest hit since 'Let It Go.'
Joe Hisaishi has composed the scores for most of Hayao Miyazaki's films, creating some of anime's most iconic music.
Illumination Entertainment, founded in 2007, has produced the Despicable Me, Minions, and Sing franchises.
Illumination's The Secret Life of Pets (2016) was inspired by the question of what pets do when their owners leave for the day.
Sing (2016) featured A-list voice actors performing real pop songs and grossed over $634 million worldwide.
The Lego Movie (2014) was snubbed for a Best Animated Feature Oscar nomination, an omission that sparked controversy.
The Lego Movie's 'Everything Is Awesome' became a viral hit and was nominated for Best Original Song.
The Croods (2013) was a DreamWorks film that spawned a sequel grossing over $215 million despite releasing during the pandemic.
Megamind (2010) was a DreamWorks film that gained a massive cult following years after its release through internet memes.
Rise of the Guardians (2012) featured mythical figures like Santa Claus, the Easter Bunny, and the Tooth Fairy as action heroes.
The Boss Baby (2017) was unexpectedly nominated for Best Animated Feature and grossed over $528 million.
Trolls (2016) and Trolls World Tour (2020) explored different music genres while featuring an ensemble voice cast led by Anna Kendrick and Justin Timberlake.
Sony Pictures Animation produced Cloudy with a Chance of Meatballs (2009), which was much more successful than expected.
Hotel Transylvania (2012) launched a franchise starring Adam Sandler as Count Dracula and grossed over $2 billion combined.
Blue Sky Studios, which produced the Ice Age franchise, was shut down by Disney in 2021 after the Fox acquisition.
The Ice Age franchise produced five feature films and grossed over $3.2 billion worldwide.
Scrat, the saber-toothed squirrel from Ice Age, became one of the most recognizable animated characters despite having no dialogue.
Warner Bros. Animation produced The Lego Batman Movie (2017), which was praised as one of the best Batman films of any format.
Paramount Animation's Teenage Mutant Ninja Turtles: Mutant Mayhem (2023) used a sketchy art style inspired by actual teenage drawings.
The animation industry employs hundreds of thousands of people worldwide, with major hubs in Los Angeles, Vancouver, Tokyo, Seoul, and Mumbai.
South Korea's animation studios do much of the outsourced production work for American TV shows like The Simpsons and Family Guy.
India's animation industry has grown rapidly, with studios contributing to both domestic productions and international outsourcing.
Walt Disney Animation Studios and Pixar Animation Studios are both owned by The Walt Disney Company but operate as separate creative entities.
Disney acquired Pixar in 2006 for $7.4 billion, making Steve Jobs Disney's largest shareholder at the time.
DreamWorks Animation was acquired by NBCUniversal in 2016 for $3.8 billion.
The term 'anime' in Japan refers to all animated works regardless of origin, though internationally it specifically means Japanese animation.
Limited animation, which uses fewer drawings per second, was popularized by studios like Hanna-Barbera to make TV animation affordable.
Hanna-Barbera created some of the most iconic TV animated characters including Yogi Bear, Scooby-Doo, and the Jetsons.
The Jetsons (1962) predicted many future technologies including smartwatches, video calls, and robotic vacuums.
ThunderCats (1985) combined American animation storytelling with Japanese anime visual styles, a precursor to many future series.
Voltron was adapted from two different Japanese anime series — Beast King GoLion and Armored Fleet Dairugger XV.
Transformers: The Movie (1986) shocked young audiences by killing off Optimus Prime in the first act.
He-Man and the Masters of the Universe (1983) was one of the first animated TV shows created primarily to sell toys.
Rugrats ran from 1991 to 2004 and spawned three theatrical films, becoming one of Nickelodeon's most successful franchises.
Dexter's Laboratory (1996) was one of Cartoon Network's first original animated series and a major hit for the network.
The Powerpuff Girls, created by Craig McCracken, debuted in 1998 and became a pop culture phenomenon.
Samurai Jack (2001) was praised for its cinematic visual storytelling and minimal dialogue.
Animaniacs (1993) blended educational content with irreverent humor and featured the popular segment 'Pinky and the Brain.'
Ren & Stimpy (1991) pushed the boundaries of TV animation with its gross-out humor and surreal art style.
The Magic School Bus (1994) made science education entertaining and was based on the book series by Joanna Cole.
Dora the Explorer debuted in 2000 and became one of the most successful preschool programs in television history.
Peppa Pig has been translated into over 40 languages and airs in more than 180 countries.
PAW Patrol has generated billions in merchandise sales since its debut in 2013.
Cocomelon became one of the most-viewed YouTube channels in history, accumulating over 150 billion views.
Arcane (2021), based on League of Legends, was produced by French studio Fortiche and became one of Netflix's highest-rated animated series.
Castlevania (2017–2021) on Netflix was one of the first successful animated series based on a video game.
Invincible (2021), based on Robert Kirkman's comic, earned acclaim for its mature storytelling and shocking violence.
Primal (2019) by Genndy Tartakovsky told a story about a caveman and a dinosaur with virtually no dialogue.
Bojack Horseman (2014–2020) was lauded for its nuanced portrayal of depression, addiction, and Hollywood culture.
Undone (2019) on Amazon Prime used rotoscoping to create a surreal, dreamlike visual style.
Love, Death & Robots (2019) is a Netflix anthology featuring different animation studios and styles for each episode.
Vivo (2021) was Sony Pictures Animation's first musical and featured songs by Lin-Manuel Miranda.
The Bad Guys (2022) was based on Aaron Blabey's children's book series and used a stylized animation approach inspired by Into the Spider-Verse.
Nimona (2023), produced by Blue Sky Studios before its closure and finished by Annapurna Pictures, was nominated for Best Animated Feature.
Elemental (2023) had Pixar's worst opening weekend but recovered significantly through strong word-of-mouth and international grosses.
Strange World (2022) was a notable box office disappointment for Disney, earning only $73 million against a $180 million budget.
Lightyear (2022) underperformed expectations despite its connection to the Toy Story franchise.
Migration (2023) by Illumination surprised analysts by grossing over $243 million worldwide.
Wish (2023) underperformed at the box office despite being Disney's 100th anniversary celebration film.
The global animation market is projected to reach over $580 billion by 2030.
Warner Bros. produced the first synchronized sound cartoon, Steamboat Willie competitor 'My Old Kentucky Home,' though Disney's version became more famous.
The Fleischer brothers' Superman cartoons (1941–1943) are considered some of the finest examples of Golden Age animation.
UPA (United Productions of America) revolutionized animation in the 1950s with limited, stylized animation in shorts like Gerald McBoing-Boing.
The nine Disney animators known as the 'Nine Old Men' defined the studio's golden age and developed the 12 principles of animation.
The 12 principles of animation, including squash and stretch, anticipation, and staging, were codified in the 1981 book 'The Illusion of Life.'
Squash and stretch is considered the most important of the 12 principles, giving animated objects a sense of weight and flexibility.
Disney's use of the 'Disney death' trope — where a character appears to die but returns — has become one of animation's most recognized storytelling devices.
The concept of 'Disney villains' has become a franchise in itself, with dedicated merchandise, theme park events, and media.
Maleficent from Sleeping Beauty is often considered Disney's greatest villain, inspiring a live-action film starring Angelina Jolie.
Scar's 'Be Prepared' in The Lion King was partly inspired by Nazi propaganda footage, with deliberate visual parallels.
Ursula from The Little Mermaid was inspired by the drag performer Divine, known for collaborating with filmmaker John Waters.
Gaston from Beauty and the Beast was added relatively late in development to give the film a more traditional villain.
The Pixar Ball and Luxo Jr. lamp are Easter eggs hidden in nearly every Pixar film.
A113 — a reference to a classroom at CalArts where many animators studied — appears as an Easter egg in most Pixar and many Disney films.
The Pizza Planet truck from Toy Story appears in some form in nearly every Pixar film.
John Lasseter, a key figure in Pixar's founding, was originally fired from Disney before revolutionizing animation at Pixar.
Ed Catmull, Pixar's co-founder, was a computer scientist whose work on texture mapping and curved surfaces laid the groundwork for CGI animation.
Pete Docter has directed four Pixar films — Monsters, Inc., Up, Inside Out, and Soul — and currently leads Pixar as Chief Creative Officer.
Andrew Stanton directed Finding Nemo and WALL-E, two of Pixar's most acclaimed and commercially successful films.
Lee Unkrich directed Toy Story 3 and Coco, both of which won the Academy Award for Best Animated Feature.
Pixar's Braintrust is a group of directors and writers who give candid feedback on films in development to improve storytelling.
DreamWorks' Shrek franchise grossed over $3.5 billion worldwide across four films.
How to Train Your Dragon: The Hidden World (2019) concluded the trilogy with worldwide earnings of $521 million.
Kung Fu Panda 4 (2024) continued the franchise after a six-year gap and earned over $545 million worldwide.
The Trolls franchise by DreamWorks has grossed over $1 billion combined across its films.
Madagascar 3: Europe's Most Wanted (2012) was the highest-grossing entry in the Madagascar franchise at $746 million.
Wallace & Gromit: The Curse of the Were-Rabbit (2005) won the Academy Award for Best Animated Feature.
Aardman Animations uses approximately 2.8 tons of plasticine for a typical feature film production.
The PES short films, including Fresh Guacamole (2012), showcase everyday objects animated as food ingredients in stop-motion.
Fresh Guacamole (2012) at under 2 minutes is the shortest film ever nominated for an Academy Award.
Marcel the Shell with Shoes On (2021) began as a short film before being expanded into a hybrid live-action/stop-motion feature.
Wes Anderson's stop-motion films use real fabrics, real fur, and practical materials rather than synthetic alternatives.
Guillermo del Toro spent over 15 years developing his stop-motion version of Pinocchio.
The rise of streaming platforms has dramatically increased demand for animated content, with Netflix, Disney+, and Apple TV+ all investing heavily.
Disney+ launched on November 12, 2019, and quickly became the primary home for Disney, Pixar, and Marvel animated content.
Netflix has significantly expanded its animated film output, releasing multiple original animated features each year since 2019.
Apple TV+'s Wolfwalkers (2020) and Luck (2022) marked the tech company's entry into animated feature films.
The Emoji Movie (2017) holds a 7% rating on Rotten Tomatoes and won the Razzie for Worst Picture.
Rotten Tomatoes' highest-rated animated film is Toy Story 2 at 100% with over 160 reviews.
Paddington 2 (2017), while live-action with CGI, held the record for Rotten Tomatoes' best-reviewed film before it was surpassed.
AnimeFest, Anime Expo, and Comiket are among the largest annual conventions celebrating anime and animation culture worldwide.
The Annecy International Animation Film Festival in France, established in 1960, is the oldest and one of the most prestigious animation festivals.
The Annie Awards, presented by the International Animated Film Association, are the animation industry's highest honor outside the Oscars.
Hayao Miyazaki has retired and come out of retirement multiple times — The Boy and the Heron marked his latest return at age 82.
Isao Takahata, co-founder of Studio Ghibli, directed Grave of the Fireflies and The Tale of the Princess Kaguya before his death in 2018.
Glen Keane, who animated Ariel, Beast, and Tarzan at Disney, later directed Over the Moon (2020) for Netflix.
Don Bluth left Disney in 1979 along with several animators, citing declining quality, and went on to create rival animated features.
Genndy Tartakovsky created Dexter's Laboratory, Samurai Jack, and the original Star Wars: Clone Wars animated series.
Masaaki Yuasa is known for his wildly experimental anime style in works like Mind Game, Devilman Crybaby, and Keep Your Hands Off Eizouken!
Satoshi Kon directed Perfect Blue, Paprika, Tokyo Godfathers, and Millennium Actress — all acclaimed for their psychological depth.
Paprika (2006) by Satoshi Kon is widely believed to have influenced Christopher Nolan's Inception.
Mamoru Hosoda directed Wolf Children, The Girl Who Leapt Through Time, and Belle, establishing himself as a leading anime filmmaker.
Princess Mononoke was the first anime film to be distributed in the US by a major studio (Miramax/Disney).
The Boy and the Heron won the Academy Award for Best Animated Feature in 2024, Miyazaki's second Oscar win.
Demon Slayer: Mugen Train became the first anime film to top the global box office for multiple consecutive weeks.
One Piece Film: Red (2022) grossed over $300 million worldwide.
Dragon Ball Super: Broly (2018) earned over $120 million and brought Dragon Ball back to global theatrical prominence.
Suzume (2022) by Makoto Shinkai grossed over $327 million worldwide, making it one of the highest-grossing anime films ever.
The original Bambi (1942) was based on a 1923 Austrian novel and was deeply controversial for its depiction of a hunter killing Bambi's mother.
Pinocchio (1940) was a technical masterpiece but was a commercial failure on initial release due to World War II reducing the international market.
Dumbo (1941) was made quickly and cheaply to help recoup losses from Pinocchio and Fantasia, and it became a box office hit.
Alice in Wonderland (1951) was not a major success on its initial release but gained popularity through TV broadcasts and the counterculture movement.
The Aristocats (1970) was the first Disney animated feature approved after Walt Disney's death.
Robin Hood (1973) reused animation from earlier Disney films to save money, a controversial practice among animation purists.
The Fox and the Hound (1981) was notable for the early work of a young Tim Burton as an animator at Disney.
The Black Cauldron (1985) was Disney's first PG-rated animated film and was a critical and commercial disappointment.
Oliver & Company (1988) modernized Oliver Twist with animals in New York City and featured Billy Joel's voice and music.
Treasure Planet (2002) was a box office bomb despite being a passion project for directors Ron Clements and John Musker.
The Princess and the Frog (2009) featured Disney's first African American princess, Tiana, and was the studio's return to hand-drawn animation.
Zootopia was praised for its sophisticated commentary on prejudice and discrimination through its animal-populated world.
Raya and the Last Dragon (2021) drew from Southeast Asian cultures and was praised for its diverse representation.
Strange World (2022) featured Disney's first openly gay teen protagonist in an animated feature.
The Haunted Mansion ride at Disneyland inspired the 2003 live-action film and has influenced countless animated Halloween specials.
Disney's Fantasia 2000 was released in IMAX theaters, making it the first animated film shown in the format.
Stop-motion animation typically requires 12 to 24 individual photographs per second of film.
Claymation, a form of stop-motion using clay figures, was popularized by Will Vinton, who trademarked the term.
The California Raisins, animated by Will Vinton Studios using claymation, became a pop culture sensation in the late 1980s.
Ray Harryhausen's stop-motion creatures in films like Jason and the Argonauts (1963) inspired generations of animators and filmmakers.
Jan Švankmajer, a Czech filmmaker, has created surreal stop-motion works that have influenced directors like Tim Burton and Terry Gilliam.
Laika's Missing Link (2019) won the Golden Globe for Best Animated Feature, beating Toy Story 4 and Frozen II.
The average Laika film uses between 40,000 and 70,000 3D-printed faces for character expressions.
ParaNorman (2012) was the first stop-motion film to use a 3D color printer for character faces, achieving unprecedented expressiveness.
In the 1930s and 1940s, animated shorts were shown before feature films in theaters, making characters like Mickey Mouse and Bugs Bunny stars.
The Looney Tunes character Bugs Bunny first appeared in the 1940 short 'A Wild Hare.'
Chuck Jones directed many of the most celebrated Looney Tunes shorts, including 'What's Opera, Doc?' and the Road Runner series.
Tex Avery, who worked at both Warner Bros. and MGM, was known for his wild, anarchic animation style and invented the 'take' gag.
The Silly Symphonies series by Disney pioneered many animation techniques, with 'Flowers and Trees' (1932) being the first full-color cartoon to win an Oscar.
Walt Disney received a special Academy Award for creating Mickey Mouse in 1932.
Heavy Metal (1981) was an adult animated anthology film based on the magazine, featuring music by Black Sabbath, Blue Öyster Cult, and others.
Ralph Bakshi's The Lord of the Rings (1978) used rotoscoping and was one of the first attempts to adapt Tolkien for film.
Rock & Rule (1983) by Nelvana was one of the first animated features produced entirely in Canada.
Belladonna of Sadness (1973) is a Japanese animated film known for its watercolor art style and was rediscovered by modern audiences decades later.
Night on the Galactic Railroad (1985) is a Japanese animated film based on Kenji Miyazawa's beloved novel, featuring anthropomorphic cats.
Nausicaä of the Valley of the Wind (1984) predates Studio Ghibli but is considered an honorary Ghibli film and led to the studio's founding.
The term 'Japanimation' was commonly used in the West before 'anime' became the preferred term in the 1990s.
Mobile Suit Gundam (1979) launched the 'real robot' subgenre of mecha anime and became a massive cultural franchise.
Dragon Ball Z's 'Frieza Saga' is one of the most iconic story arcs in anime history, with Goku's Super Saiyan transformation becoming legendary.
Naruto Shippuden ran for 500 episodes between 2007 and 2017, continuing the story of Naruto Uzumaki's path to becoming Hokage.
Boruto: Naruto Next Generations continued Naruto's legacy through his son and debuted in 2017.
One Piece has over 1,100 anime episodes, making it one of the longest-running anime series still in production.
Attack on Titan's final episode aired in 2023, concluding a story that began in 2013 and became a worldwide phenomenon.
Chainsaw Man (2022) by studio MAPPA received acclaim for its animation quality and unconventional storytelling.
MAPPA studio has become one of the most prominent anime studios, producing Attack on Titan's final season, Jujutsu Kaisen, and Chainsaw Man.
Studio Bones has produced acclaimed anime series including Fullmetal Alchemist: Brotherhood, My Hero Academia, and Mob Psycho 100.
Wit Studio animated the first three seasons of Attack on Titan before MAPPA took over for the final season.
Kyoto Animation is known for its exceptionally high animation quality in series like Violet Evergarden, K-On!, and A Silent Voice.
A Silent Voice (2016) by Kyoto Animation addressed bullying and disability with emotional sensitivity and was a box office hit in Japan.
The Kyoto Animation arson attack on July 18, 2019, was one of the deadliest mass killings in modern Japanese history, taking 36 lives.
Spy x Family became one of the most popular anime series of 2022, blending espionage, family comedy, and action.
Oshi no Ko (2023) captivated audiences with its dark exploration of Japan's entertainment industry.
The anime industry has faced criticism for overworking animators, with some earning below minimum wage despite producing globally successful content.
Pixar's short films, shown before their features, have won multiple Academy Awards, including Geri's Game, For the Birds, and Bao.
Bao (2018) was Pixar's first short directed by a woman, Domee Shi, and it won the Academy Award for Best Animated Short Film.
Disney's Paperman (2012) pioneered a technique combining CGI and hand-drawn aesthetics called Meander.
The Academy Award for Best Animated Short Film has been awarded since 1932, making it one of the oldest Oscar categories.
Luxo Jr. (1986) was the first CGI short to be nominated for an Academy Award and gave Pixar its iconic lamp mascot.
Tin Toy (1988) was the first CGI film to win an Academy Award and directly inspired the development of Toy Story.
Animation has been used extensively in advertising, from the Jolly Green Giant to the Geico Gecko and M&M's characters.
The Gorillaz are a virtual band created by Damon Albarn and Jamie Hewlett featuring four animated band members.
Music videos like A-ha's 'Take On Me' (1985) and Kanye West's 'Heartless' (2008) used rotoscoping and animation to iconic effect.
Waking Life (2001) by Richard Linklater was entirely rotoscoped over live-action footage, creating a dreamlike visual experience.
A Scanner Darkly (2006) used interpolated rotoscoping to create a surreal look matching Philip K. Dick's paranoid story.
The Rescuers Down Under (1990) was Disney's first animated sequel released theatrically and was one of the first films to use a fully digital ink-and-paint system.
CAPS (Computer Animation Production System), developed by Pixar for Disney, revolutionized how animated films were colored and composed.
The shift from hand-drawn to CGI animation at major studios accelerated after the massive success of Shrek and Finding Nemo in the early 2000s.
The Princess and the Frog (2009) was Disney's last traditionally animated theatrical feature until the style was blended into later projects.
Many animators argue that hand-drawn animation conveys emotion more naturally than CGI, citing the warmth of line work.
Anime studios often animate at 'twos' — 12 unique drawings per second — rather than 'ones' (24 drawings), for both artistic and practical reasons.
The term 'sakuga' refers to moments of exceptionally fluid and expressive animation in anime, often by star animators.
Yutaka Nakamura of Studio Bones is one of anime's most celebrity animators, known for his dynamic action sequences in My Hero Academia.
